utilityName: Sarasota County Public Utilities Cross-Connection Control Program
testingFrequency: On the county notification cycle, with annual operational testing where required
dueBasis: Sarasota County requires maximum protection for all non-residential customers, can require annual operational testing for residential irrigation assemblies, and routes notices to approved testers with shut-off timelines and hazard IDs.
coveredPropertyTypes: Residential properties with auxiliary water or separate irrigation
Residential properties with connected irrigation plumbing
County utility customers with protected assemblies
coveredDeviceTypes: Maximum protection assemblies at the meter
officialListLabel: Open the Sarasota County approved contractor list
penalties: Failure to respond may result in code enforcement action. Reports can be rejected if a contractor is not properly registered with the utility.
sourceExcerpt: Sarasota County is a high-quality Florida county utility because it combines customer notices, hazard IDs, approved contractor lists, and separate fire-protection contractor routing.
verdictSummary: Sarasota County is a strong Florida county utility because notice-based compliance, shutoff risk, and approved contractor lists all live on the same official program surface.
whoIsAffected: Sarasota County utility customers with non-residential services, auxiliary water, separate irrigation, or connected lawn irrigation that creates cross-connection risk.
residentialNotes: Residential demand is real here because the county explicitly addresses separate irrigation systems, auxiliary water, and connected lawn irrigation plumbing.
The county even offers a lower-friction dual-check option in limited situations.
commercialNotes: Commercial value is strong because all non-residential customers must maintain maximum protection at the meter.
The county keeps both general approved contractors and fire protection contractors on public official lists.

annualTesting.summary: Sarasota County uses utility notices, approved testers, and county-side enforcement to keep backflow certifications current.
highlights: Failure to respond can lead to code enforcement action.
Approved testers receive shut-off dates, service address, and hazard ID information from the customer notice.
Reports may be rejected if the contractor is not properly registered with the utility.
workflowSteps: Read the county notice and keep the shut-off date and hazard ID handy.
Choose an approved tester from the county list.
Complete the test or repair and keep the utility's accepted report current.

irrigation.summary: Sarasota County is especially strong for irrigation because it explicitly covers separate irrigation systems, connected irrigation plumbing, and auxiliary water systems.
highlights: Connected lawn irrigation must meet Florida code with PVB or RP protection.
The utility may require annual operational testing for irrigation devices.
Approved testers are public and county-registered.
workflowSteps: Confirm whether the irrigation system is separate, connected, or auxiliary-water-fed.
Choose an approved contractor from the county list.
Respond before the county notice window closes.

fireLine.summary: The official PDF includes a separate fire protection contractor list.
highlights: Fire protection contractors are listed separately from general backflow contractors.
The county warns that unregistered providers can have reports rejected.
This makes the fire-line route commercially useful and utility-authentic.
workflowSteps: Confirm the assembly is tied to fire protection.
Choose a contractor from the fire protection section of the county PDF.
Submit the accepted report before the county deadline.
Read the county notice and identify the hazard ID and shut-off date.
Choose an approved contractor from the county PDF.
Complete testing or repair on time.
Avoid code enforcement or shutoff by keeping the accepted report current.
failureHighlights: Sarasota County uses shut-off dates and hazard IDs in its notices.
Approved contractor and fire protection contractor lists are public.
Unregistered providers can have reports rejected.

costBand.testingRange: Testing is market-priced, but the county encourages customers to use an approved contractor and keep the report accepted.
costBand.repairRetestRange: Repair and retest cost rises when the first report fails or the property drifts toward shutoff.
costBand.pricingNotes: The strongest local value is the county's clear notice-to-contractor workflow and separate fire list.
